title: Rahab Hides the Two Spies
description: Joshua sent two spies secretly to scout Jericho. Rahab, a prostitute living in the city wall, hid them and helped them escape, believing Israel's God was the true God. She was promised safety for herself and her household in exchange for hanging a scarlet cord from her window.

title: Jordan River Parts as Priests Step In
description: As Israel prepared to enter Canaan, God commanded the priests carrying the ark of the covenant to step into the Jordan River at flood stage. The moment their feet touched the water, the river stopped flowing and stood in a heap, allowing all Israel to cross on dry ground.

title: Twelve Stones Memorial Set Up at Gilgal
description: After crossing the Jordan, God commanded Joshua to have twelve men — one from each tribe — take twelve stones from the riverbed. These stones were set up at Gilgal as a memorial so that future generations would remember the miraculous crossing into Canaan.

title: Israel Circumcised at Gibeath-haaraloth
description: God commanded Joshua to circumcise the Israelite men born in the wilderness, who had never been circumcised during the forty years of wandering. This act renewed the covenant sign with the generation that would take the land, and God declared the reproach of Egypt was rolled away.

title: First Passover in Canaan, Manna Ceases
description: Israel celebrated the Passover on the plains of Jericho, marking the first observance in the promised land. The day after Passover they ate the produce of Canaan, and the manna that had sustained them for forty years in the wilderness ceased, never to appear again.

title: Commander of the LORD's Army Appears to Joshua
description: Joshua encountered a man standing with a drawn sword near Jericho and asked whether he was friend or foe. The man revealed himself as the commander of the LORD's army, and Joshua fell face down in reverence. He was told to remove his sandals, for the place was holy ground.

title: Achan's Sin: Taking Devoted Things from Jericho
description: Achan of the tribe of Judah secretly took a Babylonian robe, silver, and gold from the devoted things of Jericho, which God had commanded to be destroyed. This sin caused Israel's defeat at Ai. Achan was discovered by lot, confessed, and was stoned to death with his family in the Valley of Achor.

title: The Sun Stands Still at Gibeon
description: During Joshua's battle against the five Amorite kings who attacked Gibeon, Joshua called out to God and commanded the sun to stand still and the moon to stop. The sun halted in the sky for about a full day so Israel could complete their victory, an event unparalleled before or after.

title: Southern Campaign: Joshua Conquers Five Amorite Kings
description: After defeating the five kings at Gibeon, Joshua led a sweeping campaign through southern Canaan, capturing Makkedah, Libnah, Lachish, Eglon, Hebron, and Debir. The LORD delivered each city into Israel's hands, and Joshua struck down their kings and people with the edge of the sword.

title: Northern Campaign: Hazor and Northern Kings Defeated
description: A coalition of northern Canaanite kings led by Jabin of Hazor assembled a vast army against Israel. Joshua attacked suddenly at the waters of Merom and routed them, then burned Hazor — the head of all those kingdoms — to the ground, completing the major phase of the conquest.

title: Caleb Claims Hebron as His Inheritance
description: Caleb came to Joshua reminding him of Moses' promise that the land he had spied out would be his inheritance. At eighty-five years old, Caleb declared he was still as strong as when Moses sent him. He asked for the hill country of Hebron, which Joshua granted, and Caleb drove out the Anakim.

title: Daughters of Zelophehad Claim Their Inheritance
description: The five daughters of Zelophehad — Mahlah, Noah, Hoglah, Milcah, and Tirzah — came before Eleazar the priest and Joshua claiming the inheritance their father had been promised, since he died without sons. Following the ruling God gave Moses, Joshua granted them an inheritance among their father's brothers.

title: Tabernacle Set Up at Shiloh
description: With the western portion of Canaan subdued, the whole congregation of Israel assembled at Shiloh and set up the tent of meeting there. Shiloh became the central sanctuary of Israel for the next several hundred years, housing the ark of the covenant and serving as the religious heart of the nation.

title: Joshua's Farewell Address at Shechem
description: Near the end of his life, Joshua gathered all Israel at Shechem and recounted God's faithfulness from Abraham through the conquest. He challenged the people to choose whom they would serve and renewed the covenant. The people declared, 'As for us, we will serve the LORD,' and a great stone was set as a witness.

title: Joshua Dies and Is Buried at Timnath-serah
description: Joshua son of Nun, the servant of the LORD, died at the age of one hundred and ten years. He was buried in the land of his inheritance at Timnath-serah in the hill country of Ephraim, north of Mount Gaash. Israel served the LORD throughout all the days of Joshua and the elders who outlived him.

title: Othniel Delivers Israel from Cushan-rishathaim
description: After Israel abandoned God and served the Baals, the LORD sold them into the hand of Cushan-rishathaim king of Mesopotamia for eight years. When Israel cried out, God raised up Othniel son of Kenaz, Caleb's younger brother, who was empowered by the Spirit and delivered Israel, bringing forty years of peace.

title: Ehud Kills Eglon King of Moab
description: Eglon king of Moab oppressed Israel for eighteen years. Ehud, a left-handed Benjaminite, crafted a double-edged dagger and hid it on his right thigh. He delivered tribute to Eglon, then claimed a secret message and drove the blade into the king's belly. Ehud escaped and led Israel to defeat the Moabites, bringing eighty years of peace.

title: Shamgar Kills 600 Philistines with an Ox Goad
description: Shamgar son of Anath struck down six hundred Philistines with an ox goad — a sharpened farming implement used to drive cattle. Though little else is recorded about him, he is counted among the judges of Israel and credited with saving Israel in his day.

title: Deborah Judges Israel Under the Palm Tree
description: Deborah, a prophetess, was judging Israel at a time when the nation was oppressed by Jabin king of Canaan. She held court under the Palm of Deborah between Ramah and Bethel in the hill country of Ephraim, and the Israelites came to her to have their disputes decided.

title: Jael Kills Sisera with a Tent Peg
description: After Israel's victory over the Canaanites, the enemy commander Sisera fled on foot and sought refuge in the tent of Jael, wife of Heber the Kenite. She gave him milk and covered him with a blanket, then while he slept, she drove a tent peg through his temple, fulfilling Deborah's prophecy that a woman would receive the glory for his defeat.

title: Gideon Called by the Angel of the LORD at the Winepress
description: While Gideon was secretly threshing wheat in a winepress to hide from the Midianites, the angel of the LORD appeared and called him a mighty warrior. Gideon protested his weakness but the LORD promised to be with him. When Gideon offered a sacrifice, fire consumed it from the rock, confirming God's call.

title: Gideon Destroys the Altar of Baal
description: The LORD commanded Gideon to tear down his father's altar to Baal and cut down the Asherah pole beside it, then build a proper altar to the LORD and sacrifice a bull. Gideon obeyed by night, fearing the townspeople. When they discovered it was Gideon, his father Joash defended him, saying let Baal contend for himself — earning Gideon the name Jerubbaal.

title: Gideon's 300 Defeat Midian at the Spring of Harod
description: God reduced Gideon's army from 32,000 to 300 by selecting only those who lapped water with their hand to their mouth. With trumpets, torches, and jars, the 300 surrounded the Midianite camp at night, smashing the jars and blowing their trumpets. The Midianites fled in confusion, turning their swords on each other.

title: Abimelech Kills His 70 Brothers at Shechem
description: Abimelech, an illegitimate son of Gideon, conspired with his mother's relatives at Shechem to make himself king. They gave him seventy pieces of silver from the temple of Baal-berith, which he used to hire worthless and reckless men. He then went to his father's house at Ophrah and killed seventy of his half-brothers on one stone, though the youngest, Jotham, escaped.

title: Abimelech Killed by a Millstone Thrown by a Woman
description: Abimelech besieged the city of Thebez, and when the people fled into a strong tower, he approached the tower door intending to set it on fire. A woman dropped an upper millstone on his head, cracking his skull. Mortally wounded, he quickly commanded his armor-bearer to run him through so it could not be said a woman killed him — but it was, fulfilling Jotham's curse.

title: Jephthah Defeats the Ammonites
description: Empowered by the Spirit of the LORD, Jephthah advanced against the Ammonites and struck them down from Aroer to the vicinity of Minnith — twenty towns. The Ammonites were subdued before Israel, fulfilling Jephthah's vow, though that vow would bring devastating personal consequences.

title: Jephthah's Daughter Mourns in the Mountains
description: When Jephthah returned victorious, his only daughter came out dancing to meet him. Remembering his rash vow to sacrifice whatever came through his door first, he was devastated. She asked for two months to wander the mountains with her companions weeping for her virginity. She returned and Jephthah carried out his vow; it became a custom for Israelite women to mourn her four days each year.

title: Samson Born to the Wife of Manoah
description: The angel of the LORD appeared to the barren wife of Manoah and announced she would conceive a son who was to be a Nazirite from birth. He would begin to deliver Israel from the Philistines. The angel appeared again and, after they offered a sacrifice, ascended in the flame. Samson was born and the Spirit of the LORD began to stir in him.

title: Samson's Riddle at His Wedding in Timnah
description: Samson went down to Timnah and took a Philistine wife. On the way he killed a lion with his bare hands, and later found honey in its carcass. At the wedding feast he posed a riddle about this to thirty Philistine companions. They pressured his wife until she revealed the answer, and Samson lost the bet but killed thirty men at Ashkelon to pay the wager.

title: Samson Defeats Philistines with the Jawbone at Lehi
description: When the Philistines came to arrest Samson at Lehi, the Spirit of the LORD came powerfully upon him. He picked up a fresh jawbone of a donkey and struck down a thousand Philistines with it. He called the place Ramath-lehi meaning 'Hill of the Jawbone,' and God miraculously provided water from the hollow in the jawbone.

title: Micah's Idol and the Levite Priest
description: A man named Micah in the hill country of Ephraim stole silver from his mother, then returned it and used it to make an idol and household gods. He set up a shrine and consecrated one of his sons as priest. Later he hired a wandering Levite as his personal priest, thinking this would bring God's blessing — illustrating the moral and religious chaos of the era when 'every man did what was right in his own eyes.'

title: Tribe of Dan Migrates North and Takes Laish
description: The tribe of Dan, unable to secure their assigned territory from the Philistines, sent scouts north who discovered the peaceful city of Laish. The Danites stole Micah's idol and priest, then marched north with 600 armed men and burned Laish to the ground, rebuilding it as Dan. They set up the stolen idol and established a rival worship center in the north.

title: The Levite's Concubine at Gibeah Atrocity
description: A Levite traveling through Gibeah with his concubine sought lodging, and wicked men of the city demanded to abuse him. His concubine was handed over and brutally assaulted all night. She died on the doorstep by morning. The Levite cut her body into twelve pieces and sent them throughout Israel as a call to action, triggering national outrage and civil war.

title: Civil War Against Benjamin at Gibeah and Mizpah
description: All Israel assembled at Mizpah and demanded that Benjamin hand over the wicked men of Gibeah. Benjamin refused, and civil war broke out. Israel suffered two devastating defeats before a third attack using an ambush strategy destroyed Gibeah and nearly annihilated the tribe of Benjamin — only 600 men survived.

title: Hannah Prays for a Son at Shiloh
description: Hannah, deeply distressed over her barrenness, wept and prayed before the LORD at the tabernacle in Shiloh. She vowed that if God gave her a son, she would dedicate him to the LORD all his days as a Nazirite. The priest Eli initially thought she was drunk but blessed her; Hannah left in peace, and God remembered her — Samuel was conceived.

title: Samuel's Call in the Night at Shiloh
description: While serving under Eli in the tabernacle, the young Samuel heard God call his name three times in the night. Eli realized it was the LORD speaking and instructed Samuel to respond, 'Speak, LORD, for your servant hears.' God revealed to Samuel that He would judge Eli's house because of his sons' wickedness and Eli's failure to restrain them.

title: Eli Hears of the Ark's Capture and Dies
description: A messenger ran from the battlefield to Shiloh with news of Israel's catastrophic defeat: thirty thousand soldiers killed, Eli's two sons dead, and the ark of God captured. When the ninety-eight-year-old Eli heard the ark was taken, he fell backward off his seat, broke his neck, and died. His daughter-in-law named her newborn son Ichabod, saying, 'The glory has departed from Israel.'

title: Ark Among the Philistines Causes Plague
description: The Philistines brought the ark of God to Ashdod and placed it in the temple of Dagon. The next morning Dagon's statue had fallen on its face before the ark; the second morning it was broken apart. The LORD afflicted the people of Ashdod and the surrounding region with tumors. The ark was moved to Gath and then Ekron, spreading plague wherever it went.

title: Ark Returned to Israel on a Cart with Cows
description: After seven months of plague, the Philistines sent the ark back on a new cart pulled by two nursing cows not broken to the yoke. Despite natural instinct to return to their calves, the cows headed straight to Beth-shemesh, confirming God's hand. The Levites unloaded the ark and offered the cows as a burnt offering on the great stone of Abel.

title: Samuel Calls Israel to Repentance at Mizpah
description: Samuel called Israel to Mizpah for national repentance, telling them to put away their foreign gods and commit fully to the LORD. The people fasted, confessed their sin, and drew water pouring it before the LORD. When Philistines attacked, God thundered from heaven throwing them into confusion, and Israel routed them. Samuel set up a stone called Ebenezer, meaning 'Thus far the LORD has helped us.'

title: Israel Demands a King
description: When Samuel grew old and his sons acted as corrupt judges, Israel's elders gathered at Ramah demanding a king like all the nations. Samuel was displeased and prayed; God told him the people had not rejected Samuel but had rejected God as their king. Samuel warned them of what a king would do — conscript their sons, take their fields, and tax their produce — but they refused to listen.

title: Saul Searches for Lost Donkeys, Meets Samuel
description: Saul, a tall and handsome young man from the tribe of Benjamin, was sent to search for his father's lost donkeys. After searching through the hill country without success, his servant suggested consulting the man of God — Samuel — who lived nearby. God had already told Samuel the day before that he would send the man who was to be Israel's first king.

title: Saul Anointed Privately by Samuel
description: Samuel took a flask of oil and poured it on Saul's head, kissing him and declaring that the LORD had anointed him ruler over His inheritance. Samuel gave Saul three confirming signs that would occur, including that the Spirit of God would come upon him prophesying with a group of prophets — all of which came to pass, confirming his call.

title: Saul Defeats the Ammonites at Jabesh-gilead
description: Nahash the Ammonite besieged Jabesh-gilead and threatened to gouge out every right eye of its citizens. Messengers brought the news to Saul; the Spirit of God rushed upon him in power. He mustered all Israel, marched overnight, and attacked the Ammonites in a three-pronged assault, routing them and rescuing the city. This victory confirmed Saul as king in the eyes of the people.

title: Samuel's Farewell Speech
description: Samuel addressed Israel at Gilgal, declaring his integrity throughout his years of leadership and challenging anyone to accuse him. He reviewed Israel's history of unfaithfulness and the LORD's faithful deliverances. He warned that if they and their king disobeyed, the hand of the LORD would be against them. Thunder and rain in the dry season confirmed Samuel's prophetic authority.

title: Saul Disobeys by Offering the Sacrifice Before Battle
description: As the Philistine army assembled and his troops began to scatter in fear, Saul waited seven days for Samuel. When Samuel did not come at the appointed time, Saul presumed to offer the burnt offering himself. Samuel arrived immediately after and condemned Saul's disobedience, declaring that his kingdom would not endure and that God had sought a man after His own heart.

title: Jonathan's Bold Attack at Michmash
description: Without telling his father, Jonathan and his armor-bearer climbed up to the Philistine outpost at Michmash, trusting that nothing could hinder the LORD from saving by many or by few. They killed about twenty Philistines and panic spread through the Philistine camp. God sent a great earthquake, and the Israelites who had been hiding joined the pursuit, routing the entire army.

title: Saul Disobeys the LORD Against Amalek
description: God commanded Saul to completely destroy the Amalekites, sparing nothing. But Saul spared King Agag and the best livestock, claiming he planned to sacrifice them to God. When Samuel confronted him, he spoke the defining words: 'To obey is better than sacrifice.' God rejected Saul as king, and Samuel hacked Agag to pieces before the LORD at Gilgal.

title: David Plays the Harp to Soothe King Saul
description: When the Spirit of the LORD departed from Saul, a harmful spirit tormented him. His servants suggested music as a remedy, and a servant recommended David son of Jesse, a skillful harpist and man of valor. Saul sent for David, who entered royal service; whenever the evil spirit troubled Saul, David played and the king found relief, and he loved David greatly.

title: Jonathan and David's Covenant of Friendship
description: After David's victory over Goliath, Jonathan's soul was knit to David's and he loved him as his own soul. Jonathan made a covenant with David and sealed it by giving him his robe, armor, sword, bow, and belt — symbolic acts of transferring his royal status. Their friendship became one of the most celebrated in Scripture.

title: David Flees from Saul and Goes to Nob
description: Fleeing from Saul, David went to the priest Ahimelech at Nob. He obtained the consecrated showbread for his men and the sword of Goliath, which had been kept wrapped behind the ephod. This act of eating the holy bread would later be cited by Jesus to illustrate that human need can supersede ceremonial law.

title: David in the Cave of Adullam, 400 Men Join Him
description: David escaped to the cave of Adullam where his brothers and his father's household joined him. Then everyone who was in distress, in debt, or discontented gathered to him — about 400 men — and he became their leader. This ragtag band would become the core of David's mighty men and eventually his royal army.

title: David and Abigail
description: David's men had protected Nabal's flocks in the wilderness and David requested provisions, but the wealthy and surly Nabal refused. David prepared to attack in anger. Nabal's wise and beautiful wife Abigail learned of it and quickly brought generous provisions, intercepting David and persuading him not to avenge himself. When Nabal died of shock ten days later, David sent for Abigail and she became his wife.

title: Saul Visits the Witch of Endor
description: Before the battle of Mount Gilboa, Saul disguised himself and visited a medium at Endor in desperation, having received no word from God through prophets or dreams. The medium called up Samuel's spirit, who declared that because Saul had disobeyed God, Israel would be defeated and Saul and his sons would join Samuel in death the next day.

title: Ish-bosheth vs David: War Between the Two Houses
description: Abner son of Ner made Saul's son Ish-bosheth king over Israel at Mahanaim, while David reigned over Judah in Hebron. The two sides met at the pool of Gibeon where Abner proposed a contest of twelve men each. The contest dissolved into full battle; Abner's men were defeated but Abner killed Joab's brother Asahel in flight, sowing seeds of future vengeance.
